sentences.text: The liquidation of the family camp, the death of my father, sending my mother into the unknown, and my transfer with 90 other boys to a new environment was really a watershed in my life.
I was suddenly alone, without my parents.
I still carried some hope that, when this all ends, my mother will come back.
I knew that my father perished.
But the hope of someday being reunited with my mother helped me to a great degree to survive in subsequent conditions, which became worse and worse as time passed by.
The camps became-- the condition-- general conditions in the camps became worse. primarily.
And the realization that I am alone was not very comforting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was, then, not quite 14 years old.

There-- one reason that we were able to survive is that there was a great deal of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] reliance on each other and help and the feeling that we are in the same boat and that we need to support each other.
Out of the 89 boys that were selected by Mengele on July 4, about 35 [SIGHS] or 40 are known to have survived the subsequent nine months, eight months.
Two thirds died in those following months, from-- mostly from starvation and disease.
So I was, again, lucky that I not only was selected to-- to live but to survive the next period.
What were you doing there?
What kind of work were you doing?
As I mentioned earlier, of the 89 boys was selected for lighter works.
So this was, again, a change that, before we were really not given specific tasks.
Now, we all had to work.
The majority was used to pull wagons.
They were-- there was a lack of horses.
So humans had to pull wagons.
And the boys did that, because all able-bodied men were sent out to work on road-work or some heavy-- carrying stones or doing something for the-- constructing the camp or the war effort.
A few of us were selected to be messengers or to help in the support services.

For instance, the camp needed-- even though, minimally-- needed electricians to-- just to keep the barbed-wires electrified or to repair roofs or to distribute blankets, if there were any, or clothing.
Collect clothing from those who arrived.
Effect There was a huge-- well, huge.

There was a group that was charged with helping in the gas chambers that had to push people into the gas chambers and then carry out the dead bodies and burn them and extract the gold teeth and cut the hair and use any conceivable raw material for the war effort.

There were those who had to go to the ramp when the train arrived and sort out all the luggage, pick out-- throw toothbrushes on one pile and suitcases in another and false teeth and et cetera, et cetera.
So there were many ways of employing people.

I was a messenger for a department that supplied all sorts of utensils, either for the Germans or for the prisoners in better positions-- I don't know-- blankets, shoes, various stuff that was stored in the barrack.
And I also carried messages from the various-- between the various camps.
So I had to go through various check-points and salute the SS guards and give my number-- because names didn't exist, we were all just numbers-- and then proceed to my destination.
Was this considered a better position than--
Yes.
In that-- in that position, I was able, a number of times, to go to camps which had people who just arrived.
And on such occasion, a man who lived in our town-- several men were there-- and I was able to bring them some soup.
One of these men, later on, became my guardian after the war.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] We-- we were placed together for the-- for several months in this block, which half of it was for a punishment cell.
And we had to hear and, sometimes, see horrible torture of human beings.
Particular memory are the prisoners who tried to escape and who were brought back and slowly killed by [BACKROUND NOISE] horrible beating and other punishment.
And then, when they were-- you know-- 90% dead, they were publicly hanged.
And everybody had to come and witness the hanging.
Public beatings were often a mandatory spectacle as well.
And the neighboring block had this-- what was called a special commando, Sonderkommando, which worked in the gas chambers.

And they would, in a way, have a privileged position, because they could steal cigarettes and food from the freshly-arrived people or sometimes even gold teeth that they exchanged for food with the SS and did all sorts of business.
On the other hand, they knew that every few months they themselves would be driven into the gas chamber, so that they could not tell what they saw to the next generation, so to the next group.
So it was-- you know-- like, knowing to be on death-row and performing horrible tasks.

There was, during our stay there, a revolt of that Sonderkommando, where they were able to get some rifles and armed themselves with all sorts of pipes and so on and staged an uprising in one of the crematorias.
But they were all killed eventually by the overwhelming superiority of SS.
But they were able to injure or kill a few guards.
But that's all.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Strangely enough, I don't really remember too much about other of the 89 boys.
Just, at that time, that six months is-- is a somewhat of a blur.

I remember, where I worked, that there was some only Poles who were my superior prisoners-- that the Poles really hated Jews and that the Jews were the lowest of the lowest, except the Russian war prisoners, who were treated much worse than anybody else.
And they were tortured and not given any food.
Well, there was a hierarchy in the camp-- that the most privileged were the German-- the real criminals who started Auschwitz.
They had black triangles.
There were-- every group, or-- or nationality or religion, had their special signs.
Jews had their Yellow Star of David.
Yes, yes.
Right.
They were-- these were not Jewish Poles.

Not these that I worked with in-- in the-- in the family camp, there were only Jews from Theresienstadt, which was 90% Czechs-- some Germans, some Dutch-- very few Germans, very few Dutch, but mostly Czechs.
Slowly, we were taken out of this barrack number 13 and placed in-- amongst the other people in the camp.
And as labor needs occurred in other camps, some of the boys-- this 89-- were shipped elsewhere, either in Auschwitz I, the main camp, or to completely elsewhere.
So the group dwindled.

Then, as the Russian Front advanced in December and January from the east, the Germans were sending more and more [BACKROUND NOISE] people to the West, to the-- to the German heartland, to liquidate the camps, to leave-- try and eradicate the evidence of what they have created.
Late in 1944, they stopped using the gas chambers and the crematorium.
And shortly before everybody left, they actually blew up a couple of the crematoria and gas chambers.
I was still working as a messenger, to the best of my memory.
But as winter intensified and the Front advanced, more and more people were being sent out.
And there was a panic, and fear, that those who will stay behind will all be shot.
And indeed, only-- eventually, only the very, very-- the people who just couldn't move were left behind.
And the person who was later my guardian was one of them who was left there.
Because he injured both his feet and couldn't-- couldn't even get out of his bed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- in a hurry one night, we were all herded together and sent out in-- in groups into the night.
And it was very, very cold.
Because I had a job that had access to clothing, I had reasonably serviceable shoes, whereas many of the prisoners had wooden shoes.
They had no socks and were ill-equipped for the freezing night and-- and the snow.
The death march started somewhat disorganized.
And there were-- as I said, we were sent out in groups and--
They didn't tell us anything.
I had no idea where we were going, how long we'll be going.
Here and there, I would find or be together with somebody I knew from this group.
But generally speaking, I seem to have the image that I was more or less on my own.
Because despite the fact that I had reasonably pair of shoes than the average, I was lagging behind.
And I could hear the shots.
Because whoever couldn't walk fast enough was-- and lagged behind-- was shot.
And the whole road was lined with dead bodies with completely fractured heads.
The-- the SS guards would put the guns close to your head and completely blow you.
Most of them were walking you know, along and behind.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were three days and two nights or three nights and two days on-- on this horrible journey.
One night, we stayed in some barns and the next night in some open space.
And there was this-- one of the horrible feelings was that we were crossing the countryside where people lived normal lives.
And we were passing homes where people slept in normal beds and set their tables and ate food and had families.
And I think there was always this feeling of being trapped and being different and helpless situation, while the rest of the world was unwilling to recognize the situation.
This was-- this-- I'm not talking here in generalities.
General This was the sort of frustrating feeling that followed me for quite a while.
And also is-- later on, became well-known that the allies were unwilling to recognize the need either to bomb the crematoria or to do something about it.
But the population was totally apathetic and would not, in any way, extend a helping hand.
I don't remember that we encountered any people.
Because the Germans closed all the roads so there wouldn't be anybody walking either against us or with us.
But the third day, when we reached [?
Liskow ?] where we were put on open railway car carriages-- you know, like transporting goods or coal-- the train then started rolling.
And we went through little towns and stations.
And people that were around were completely apathetic and ignored us totally.
In contrast, when the train reached across the border into Bohemia, now Czech Republic, in Moravska Ostrava, which is a large industrial town, we must have either changed engines or had to wait.
People-- and this was early in the morning.
The railway workers and people who were passing by would all take out their lunches and threw it into the carriages.
And we-- those who were lucky of us who would catch it, at least had a few bites of bread or a snack.
It was-- it was a marked-- it was like day and night.
They--
I think that-- that Poles were essentially feeling that maybe that's great that all these Jews are being annihilated.
Whereas the Czechs were much more sympathetic and soft-hearted and not anti-Semitic.
And not that there were not anti-Semitic Czechs.
But the majority was not that bad.
Yes.
Yes.
One of the group's-- one of the 89 had a father who was interned in Auschwitz three years earlier.
He was-- he was sent to Auschwitz for punishment early on in the war.
And he was an electrician.
And he was able to move a little more freely, because he was a skilled worker and worked for the Germans.
So he-- when his family arrived in Auschwitz, he was trying to help them.
And his son was three years younger than I am and was very small at that time.
But he was able to save him from certain death in that first transport that went to the gas chamber on March 7, by hiding him in the hospital compound.
He was actually, somehow, hidden in the rafters.
So that was his first escape.
He also managed, somehow, either to bribe Mengele or get him into that group of 89 children, even though he would not have normally passed muster, because he was so small.
So then, he did the death-- they did the death march together.
And they ended up in the same carriage that we were.
And while the carriage was waiting in Moravska Ostrava-- that was the first [INAUDIBLE] in-- in the Czech Republic, they jumped out and were able to-- because they were from that town.
And they were able to reach the house of a friend.
But they knew that this was a house with two apartments-- that one of the bells was their friend's and the other bell was a Nazi sympathizer.
And they were faced with the choice of life and death.
And they were lucky and pushed the right button.
And they were hidden for the rest of the war.
And his name is Dorf Kulcher And he is a professor at-- in Jerusalem.
And his father just died.
His father was one of the main authors of some of the books [BACKROUND NOISE] describing Auschwitz after the war and devoted his life to the Holocaust.
But his mother, the wife of this person, was shipped to Stutthof with my mother.
And they both died there.
And only two years ago, they went to this camp and were able to find the card catalogue that the Germans kept there.
And that's how I learned the day of my mother's birth-- my mother's death.
We then-- the train then took us across Bohemia from a north-to-south direction into Austria.
And one morning, the train stopped.
And we were facing the famous quarries and fortress-- Mauthausen, which was another very-much-feared concentration camp.
It was different from Auschwitz.
We wanted to leave.
But in many respects, it was even worse, as far as the way people were killed there.
We-- I don't think we got anything to eat during those two days.
And many people died-- primarily froze to death, who was standing up in this carriage.
And then we were marched from the railway station to the-- to the camp-- to the concentration camp Mauthausen.
Well, we assumed that that was the destination.
But we learned that only once we arrived there.
I mean, one could read the name of the station.
That's why we knew we were in Mauthausen.
The [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] treatment there-- we were now in totally new, unknown environment.
We, again, were the-- the Jewish underdogs.
And we were given new numbers.
Instead of the tattooed numbers, they had a different numbering system.
And we got a little metal plate attached with a wire to our arm with a new number.
And we were, again, sent to cold showers-- this was still in the bitter window-- winter-- end of January-- and given pretty bad clothing and herded into another barrack.
We were there only a short period.
At that time, it was very difficult to-- for me to really measure time.
And everything seemed considerably longer that it actually was.
Just, the-- the time didn't pass fast enough.
It was the-- the hunger, the uncertainty, and the-- the disorientation just wouldn't let you forget.
It was-- so when I later on re-constructed those-- those months, I was surprised that what seemed like eternity was only a week or a few days, particularly the last days of the war seemed eternal.
This first time, we were there maybe a week, maybe 10 days only.
We were just inspected and cleaned and de-loused and had to wait in different places and shifted from place to place.
Until finally, we were marched back to the railway station and shipped to another destination.
I would say that maybe 20, 25 in that transport.
Others went elsewhere.
It doesn't mean that they were all dead by then.
I would say, no.
They-- they just went to other-- some went to Buchenwald, some went to Bergen-Belsen and other camps.
So would-- would--
